topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The shared coffee break was a tightrope walk. Sarah stared at Mark, the way his eyes flickered towards the window, a nervous tic she’d never seen before. "Did you ever go to Camp Whispering Pines?" she asked, the words tasting brittle on her tongue. Mark visibly stiffened, his hand tightening around his mug until his knuckles were white. "Never," he replied, his voice unnaturally level. But the way he avoided her gaze, darting glances toward the water cooler, told a different story. A cold knot formed in Sarah’s stomach, a feeling that something was fundamentally misaligned with reality. She started checking the office security feed, a pointless exercise, just to confirm that the strange feeling wasn't entirely manufactured by her own frayed nerves.

[story 2]

I kept glancing over at David, my jaw constantly clenched. Every time he laughed at something trivial, it sounded too loud, too performative. It had to be coincidence, a statistical anomaly, but the sheer weight of the possibility pressed down on my chest. "Camp Summer Jamboree?" I managed, my voice coming out a little too sharp. David blinked, a slow, deliberate movement that seemed to assess me, cataloging my reaction. He offered a small, tight smile that didn't reach his eyes. "I've never been," he said, his tone carefully neutral. Yet, as he turned back to his screen, I felt a distinct chill crawl up my spine. The air in the breakroom suddenly felt thin, charged with an unspoken secret that only I seemed capable of perceiving.

[story 3]

He kept rearranging the sugar packets on his desk, a repetitive, obsessive motion. I watched him, my breath catching in my throat. When I finally spoke, my voice was barely a whisper, "Whispering Pines?" His head snapped up, his eyes wide, a flicker of something intense crossing his features before he masked it with a practiced nonchalance. "What are you talking about?" he asked, his tone laced with an edge I couldn't quite decipher. He started pacing the small space between our desks, his movements jerky. I gripped the edge of my monitor, my knuckles aching. Every shadow in the fluorescent lighting seemed to shift, and I was certain that whatever he was hiding, it was far more significant than a simple shared memory.
